medications.indication_rationale: The likely causative agent should not simply be continued without action, but the AI Doctor should not independently instruct outright cessation without prescriber involvement — advise the patient to contact their prescribing doctor promptly to discuss stopping/switching, rather than abruptly self-managing a prescribed antihypertensive alone.
contraindications_in_this_case: Strongly suspected cause of the presenting cough — continuing unchanged without review is not appropriate

medications.indication_rationale: ARBs act on the same renin-angiotensin pathway for blood pressure control but do not inhibit ACE and therefore do not cause bradykinin accumulation — first-line substitute for patients who develop ACE inhibitor cough. This should be arranged with the prescribing doctor, not self-initiated by the patient.

Patient has already tried OTC cough syrup without benefit, consistent with ACE inhibitor cough being resistant to standard antitussives — reinforces that this is a drug-effect cough and that symptomatic treatment without addressing the causative medication is not the correct primary management.
contraindications_in_this_case: Already trialled without effect — supports the medication-cause hypothesis rather than ongoing symptomatic treatment

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not simply stop the perindopril tonight without speaking to your prescriber — contact your GP promptly (within 24-48 hours) to arrange a safe switch to an alternative blood-pressure medication.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoids both the risk of uncontrolled hypertension from abrupt unplanned cessation without a replacement plan, and the ongoing harm of ACE inhibitor cough continuing while awaiting a routine appointment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: In the meantime, try sleeping propped up on extra pillows to reduce the nocturnal worsening of the cough while you wait for your medication review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces symptom severity and improves sleep quality as a temporising measure only, not a substitute for addressing the causative medication.

patient_education_points.point: The blood pressure medication you started a few weeks ago (perindopril) commonly causes this exact kind of dry, tickly cough in some people — it happens because of a chemical build-up in the airways, not because of an infection or anything more serious.

Please contact your GP within the next day or two to talk about switching to a different blood pressure tablet that won't cause this cough — please don't just stop it yourself tonight without a plan, even though I understand how exhausted you are.
The cough should settle within one to four weeks after the medication is stopped or switched.
If you develop swelling of your lips, tongue, face, or throat, or sudden difficulty breathing, that is a different and more serious reaction — stop the medication immediately and call 000.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Chest auscultation to formally exclude other causes, blood pressure check, and arrangement of an alternative antihypertensive agent (e.g. an ARB)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If breathlessness at rest, chest pain, fever, haemoptysis, or facial/throat swelling develop before the scheduled review, seek urgent or emergency care as appropriate rather than waiting.

must_include_items: Explanation that the cough is very likely caused by the ACE inhibitor (perindopril) started a few weeks ago
Advice to contact the prescriber/GP promptly (within 1-2 days) to arrange stopping/switching the medication rather than self-managing entirely alone
Screening for and documentation of negative red flags (dyspnoea, chest pain, fever, haemoptysis, ankle swelling) before closing the diagnostic loop
Safety-netting for angio-oedema (facial/throat swelling, difficulty breathing) as a distinct, more urgent ACE inhibitor reaction
acceptable_alternatives: Advising the patient to stop the ACE inhibitor immediately AND contact the GP the same/next day is an acceptable alternative, provided safety-netting and follow-up are included
Recommending telehealth GP follow-up instead of in-person is acceptable if examination can be reasonably deferred, provided the plan still includes timely review
errors_of_omission: No mention of the medication as the likely cause
No advice to seek prescriber review for medication switch
No screening for cardiopulmonary red flags (dyspnoea, chest pain, fever, oedema, haemoptysis)
errors_of_commission: Instructing the patient to simply stop the ACE inhibitor with no follow-up plan or safety-netting at all
Recommending emergency department attendance or ambulance for this classic, low-risk presentation (over-triage)
Recommending only symptomatic cough treatment (further OTC linctus) without addressing the causative medication
Failing to distinguish ACE inhibitor cough from angio-oedema and therefore missing the need for separate safety-netting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a low-acuity (T1-T2) case. A response that recommends emergency escalation is an over-triage error; a response that fails to identify the medication cause or provide a follow-up plan within 24-48 hours is an under-triage/omission error. The passing response identifies the likely cause, screens red flags, and arranges timely (not emergency) prescriber-led medication review.
